title: A Woman of No Importance
description: A Woman of No Importance — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "A Woman of No Importance" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of contents for A Woman of No Importance'

memes.label: "Act I"
memes.description: "First act: The terrace at Hunstanton Chase. Lady Caroline, Miss Worsley, and guests converse."

memes.label: "Act II"
memes.description: "Second act: The drawing-room at Hunstanton Chase. Lord Illingworth and Mrs. Arbuthnot are introduced."

memes.label: "Act III"
memes.description: "Third act: The hall at Hunstanton Chase. Revelations and confrontations unfold."

memes.label: "Act IV"
memes.description: "Fourth act: Sitting-room in Mrs. Arbuthnot's house at Wrockley. The resolution."

memes.label: "Alice"
memes.description: "Mrs. Arbuthnot's maid at Wrockley — the only servant who belongs to the play's moral center rather than its aristocratic periphery."

memes.label: "Ambition"
memes.description: "The role of ambition in Wilde's play — the drive to rise that powers Gerald Arbuthnot's story and the social machinery of patronage that enables and constrains it."

memes.label: American vs English Values
memes.description: "The cultural contrast between American Puritan idealism and English aristocratic cynicism, embodied in the conflict between Hester Worsley and the English characters."

memes.label: Appearance vs Reality
memes.description: "The tension between surface appearance and hidden reality — a central Wildean theme that structures both the dialogue and the plot of A Woman of No Importance."

memes.label: "Archdeacon Daubeny"
memes.description: "The benign but ineffectual clergyman — representing the Established Church as a social institution that blesses the status quo without challenging its injustices."

memes.label: "Beauty"
memes.description: "Wilde's aesthetic philosophy as dramatised in A Woman of No Importance — beauty as a moral category, as social currency, and as the central value in the dandy's worldview."

memes.label: "Class and Society"
memes.description: "Wilde's portrayal of the English class system in A Woman of No Importance — where social position determines moral standing and where the boundary between the aristocracy and those who serve them is both absolute and absurd."

memes.label: "Class Consciousness"
memes.description: "The awareness of class boundaries that pervades Wilde's play — who knows their place, who transgresses it, and what happens when class lines are crossed."

memes.label: "Concealment"
memes.description: "The practice and consequences of concealment in Wilde's play — how the suppression of truth governs the lives of every major character and structures the play's society."

memes.label: "Conversation and Wit"
memes.description: "The art of conversation as the central social ritual of Wilde's dramatic world — where wit is power, talk is performance, and the drawing room becomes an arena of verbal combat."

memes.label: "Courtship and the Marriage Market"
memes.description: "The social rituals of courtship as depicted in the play — the marriage market that governs relations between young men and women."

memes.label: "Dandyism"
memes.description: "The figure of the dandy as Wilde's vehicle for social critique — Lord Illingworth as the aristocratic wit whose elegance masks moral emptiness."

relations.label: Twenty years of romance make a woman look like a ruin

memes.label: "Dedication"
memes.description: "Dedication to Gladys, Countess de Grey (Marchioness of Ripon)."

memes.label: "Dramatis Personae"
memes.description: "Cast of characters in A Woman of No Importance."

memes.label: "Duty"
memes.description: "The contested concept of duty in Wilde's play — what one owes to society, to family, and to oneself, and how these obligations conflict."

memes.label: "Education"
memes.description: "The role of education and intellectual culture in Wilde's play — classical references, literary allusions, and the educational background that marks class distinctions."

memes.label: "English Society"
memes.description: "The structure and values of English high society as depicted in A Woman of No Importance — a world of country houses, dinner parties, and rigid social codes."

memes.label: "Every saint has a past, and every sinner has a future"
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's epigram in Act III collapsing the moral distinction between saint and sinner — one of Wilde's most quotable paradoxes on the universality of human fallibility."
aliases: every saint has a past quote

memes.label: "Family"
memes.description: "The institution of the family as examined in Wilde's play — the bonds of blood, the claims of parenthood, and the question of what parents owe their children."

memes.label: "Farquhar"
memes.description: "The butler at Hunstanton Chase — a silent, efficient presence who embodies the servant class that enables aristocratic leisure."

memes.label: "Forgiveness"
memes.description: "Central moral theme in Oscar Wilde's A Woman of No Importance — the question of whether society's fallen women can or should be forgiven, and who has the right to grant or withhold forgiveness."

memes.label: "Francis"
memes.description: "A footman at Hunstanton Chase — the lowest-ranked servant visible on stage, emblematic of the invisible labour that sustains the country-house world."

memes.label: "Gender and Power"
memes.description: "The distribution and exercise of power between men and women in Wilde's play — who has it, who lacks it, and how the powerless resist."

memes.label: "Gender Roles"
memes.description: "Wilde's critique of Victorian gender ideology — where the 'separate spheres' doctrine confines women to domestic morality while men enjoy liberty without accountability."

memes.label: "Gerald Arbuthnot"
memes.description: "The illegitimate son of Lord Illingworth and Mrs. Arbuthnot — a young man on the threshold of a career whose discovery of his parentage forces a choice between social ambition and filial loyalty."

memes.label: "The Governess"
memes.description: "The figure of the governess in Victorian society and in Wilde's play — a liminal figure poised between classes, whose ambiguous social position made her both necessary and threatening to the Victorian household."

memes.label: "Hester Worsley"
memes.description: "The young American visitor whose Puritan moral convictions provide the ethical counterweight to English cynicism — the outsider who names the hypocrisy that the insiders cannot see."

memes.label: "Hunstanton Chase"
memes.description: "The country estate of Lady Hunstanton and the sole setting of A Woman of No Importance — a microcosm of English aristocratic society where the entire drama unfolds across its terrace, drawing-room, and hall."

memes.label: Hunstanton Drawing-Room
memes.description: "The drawing-room at Hunstanton Chase where Act II takes place — an interior space of heightened social performance and the play's most concentrated wit."

memes.label: Hunstanton Hall
memes.description: "The hall at Hunstanton Chase where Act III takes place — a transitional space where the play's secrets begin to surface."

memes.label: Hunstanton Terrace
memes.description: "The terrace and lawn at Hunstanton Chase where Act I takes place — the play's opening setting, a space of social display and concealed tension."

memes.label: "Hypocrisy"
memes.description: "The gap between public morality and private behaviour that Wilde identifies as the defining feature of English society — embodied in the treatment of men versus women under the sexual double standard."

memes.label: "Illegitimacy and Reputation"
memes.description: "The central plot mechanism — how a single act, judged unequally by gender, can define a life, and how the fear of scandal maintains the Victorian social order."

memes.label: "Innocence and Experience"
memes.description: "The movement from innocence to experience as a central dramatic trajectory in Wilde's play — embodied in Gerald Arbuthnot's discovery of his origins and Hester Worsley's encounter with English society."

memes.label: "Lady Caroline Pontefract"
memes.description: "The self-appointed guardian of social propriety — a comic figure whose rigid moral judgments provide some of the play's most pointed satire of upper-class hypocrisy."

memes.label: "Lady Hunstanton"
memes.description: "The good-natured hostess of Hunstanton Chase — the embodiment of English aristocratic convention whose kindness masks a complete complicity in the social system the play indicts."

memes.label: "Lady Stutfield"
memes.description: "A minor but memorable character whose breathless manner and naive questions provide comic relief while inadvertently exposing the emotional realities beneath the drawing-room banter."

memes.label: "London"
memes.description: "The distant world of London society — the source of Lord Illingworth's power and prestige, the arena of dinner-party dominance, and the world from which Mrs. Arbuthnot has been permanently excluded."

memes.label: "Lord Alfred Rufford"
memes.description: "A minor aristocratic dandy mentioned in Act I — a characteristic Wildean 'offstage gentleman' who exists only as a name on Lady Hunstanton's lips."

memes.label: "Lord Illingworth"
memes.description: "The play's antagonist — a brilliant, amoral aristocratic dandy whose past seduction of Rachel Arbuthnot and present relationship with his illegitimate son Gerald drive the entire drama."

relations.label: Twenty years of romance make a woman look like a ruin

memes.label: "Love and Romance"
memes.description: "Wilde's treatment of romantic love as both a genuine human emotion and a social performance — the play's multiple perspectives on the relationship between love, desire, marriage, and self-interest."

memes.label: Male Power and Patriarchy
memes.description: "The structures of male power in Victorian society as exposed and critiqued in A Woman of No Importance."

memes.label: "Marriage and Courtship"
memes.description: "Wilde's satirical treatment of marriage as a social institution in A Woman of No Importance — where matrimony is alternately a marketplace, a trap, and a mockery, and where the marriage plot is deconstructed rather than fulfilled."

relations.label: Twenty years of romance make a woman look like a ruin

memes.label: "Money"
memes.description: "The role of money in Wilde's social world — as a source of power, a measure of worth, and a factor that complicates gender dynamics."

memes.label: "Moral Responsibility"
memes.description: "The question of moral responsibility in Wilde's play — who owes what to whom, and whether the powerful can ever be held accountable for the harm they cause."

memes.label: "Motherhood"
memes.description: "The central emotional anchor of A Woman of No Importance — Mrs. Arbuthnot's identity as a mother defines her moral choices and her ultimate refusal to trade her hard-won maternal authority for social respectability."

memes.label: "Mr. Kelvil"
memes.description: "The earnest but ineffectual reforming MP — a minor character who embodies Wilde's satire of progressive politics that is more concerned with rhetoric than with genuine change."

memes.label: "Mrs. Allonby"
memes.description: "The female dandy of the play — a sharp-witted society woman whose epigrams rival Lord Illingworth's and whose cynicism about marriage and men provides some of the play's most memorable dialogue."

memes.label: "Mrs. Arbuthnot"
memes.description: "The play's protagonist — the 'woman of no importance' whose moral strength, forged through twenty years of suffering and motherhood, ultimately triumphs over the aristocratic privilege that wronged her."

relations.label: I was a woman of no importance

memes.label: "Mrs. Arbuthnot's Cottage"
memes.description: "Mrs. Arbuthnot's sitting room at Wrockley — the setting of Act IV, a domestic space whose privacy and moral gravity contrast with the public world of Hunstanton Chase."

memes.label: Parent-Child Relationships
memes.description: "The web of parent-child bonds — acknowledged and secret, nurturing and destructive — that drives the plot of A Woman of No Importance."

memes.label: "The Past and Present"
memes.description: "The relationship between past and present as the central dramatic engine of A Woman of No Importance — how the suppressed past returns, how memory shapes identity, and whether the present can ever escape what has gone before."
aliases: coming to terms with the past

memes.label: "Privilege"
memes.description: "The unearned advantages of birth and gender — embodied in Lord Illingworth's effortless social dominance and immunity from consequence."

memes.label: "Puritanism and the American Critique"
memes.description: "Hester Worsley's American Puritan perspective as a dramatic foil — the New World moral absolutism that exposes Old World hypocrisy while revealing its own limitations."

memes.label: "The book of life begins with a man and a woman in a garden"
memes.description: "Mrs. Allonby's epigram on the book of life — Wilde's compressed comic theology of gender and paradise."

memes.label: "It is perfectly monstrous the way people go about nowadays saying things against one behind one's back"
memes.description: "Lady Caroline Pontefract's unconsciously self-satirizing remark on gossip — a perfect Wildean comedy of manners in miniature."

memes.label: "Children begin by loving their parents."
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's famous observation on the evolution of filial love — a Wildean psychology of family relations."

memes.label: "Children begin by loving their parents"
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's aphorism on the arc of filial love — one of Wilde's most quoted observations on family life, with devastating dramatic irony."

memes.label: "Curious thing, plain women are always jealous of their husbands, beautiful women never are!"
memes.description: "Mrs. Allonby's observation on the relationship between beauty, jealousy, and social power — a witticism that encodes a sharp class and gender observation."

memes.label: "Don't call them the other sex. There is no such thing."
memes.description: "Mrs. Allonby on the essential oneness of the sexes — a Wildean paradox that challenges Victorian gender essentialism."

memes.label: "I think to elope is cowardly. It's running away from danger. And danger has become so rare in modern life."
memes.description: "Mrs. Allonby's epigram on elopement and the scarcity of danger in modern life — a dandy's complaint about bourgeois safety."

memes.label: "English women conceal their feelings till after they are married. They show them then."
memes.description: "Lady Caroline's epigram on English marital customs — a satirical observation on the emotional economy of Victorian marriage."

memes.label: "After a good dinner one can forgive anybody"
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's cynical epigram reducing forgiveness to a matter of digestive comfort — Wilde at his most brilliantly subversive."

memes.label: "What name shall I give at the club?"
memes.description: "Gerald Arbuthnot's request for his father's name."

memes.label: "God's law is only Love."
memes.description: "Hester Worsley's declaration of her moral creed."

memes.label: "So hideous a mockery as a marriage"
memes.description: "Mrs. Arbuthnot's climactic refusal of Lord Illingworth's proposal — the moral climax of the entire play."

memes.label: "The history of women is the history of the worst form of tyranny"
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's sweeping epigram on gender and power — one of Wilde's most politically charged bons mots, turning the conventional wisdom of patriarchal oppression on its head."

memes.label: "The ideal man should always talk to us as if we were goddesses"
memes.description: "Mrs. Allonby's witty prescription for the ideal man — Wilde's comedy of gender expectations compressed into a single epigram."

memes.label: "\"The Ideal Man should talk to us as if we were goddesses\""
memes.description: "Mrs. Allonby's celebrated portrait of the Ideal Man — a satirical catalogue of masculine perfection that is also a coded description of Lord Illingworth."

memes.label: "Kind is a dreadful word."
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's dismissal of 'kind' as a descriptor — a dandy's rejection of comfortable moral language."

memes.label: "You had better go and put on your overshoes at once."
memes.description: "Lady Caroline's comic obsession with her husband's health — a running joke that reveals the domestic dynamics of the Pontefract marriage."

memes.label: "Wonderfully cheerful, wonderfully cheerful."
memes.description: "Lady Hunstanton's signature mode of social management — relentless cheerfulness as a way of avoiding uncomfortable truths."

memes.label: "\"You must leave my life with me. I will not accept you.\""
memes.description: "Mrs. Arbuthnot's climactic refusal of Lord Illingworth's marriage proposal — the play's most powerful assertion of female moral autonomy."

memes.label: "Let it be said of us that we have made the world a little better for our having lived in it"
memes.description: "Hester Worsley's moral pronouncement — the play's most earnest statement of ethical purpose, and the standard against which Wilde measures his more cynical characters."

memes.label: "Nothing should be out of the reach of hope. Life is a hope."
memes.description: "Hester Worsley's moral pronouncement on hope — a statement of the American Puritan ideal that contrasts with English aristocratic cynicism."

memes.label: "What harm is there in a kiss?"
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's defense of his attempted kiss of Hester — the predator's logic in a single, devastating line."

memes.label: "Twenty years of romance make a woman look like a ruin"
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's famous epigram comparing romance and marriage — one of Wilde's most quoted lines on the institution of matrimony, and a statement that encapsulates Illingworth's moral bankruptcy."

relations.label: After a good dinner one can forgive anybody

memes.label: "Men become old, but they never become good."
memes.description: "Lady Caroline's verdict on male character — a rare moment of clear-sightedness from the play's most conventional figure."

memes.label: "Moderation is a fatal thing. Nothing succeeds like excess."
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth on the virtues of excess — a Wildean inversion of the Victorian celebration of moderation."

memes.label: "It is perfectly monstrous the way people go about saying things against one behind one's back that are absolutely and entirely true."
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's epigram on gossip — a Wildean paradox that concedes the truth of slander while mocking its social form."

memes.label: "There are far more things forbidden to us than are forbidden to them."
memes.description: "Mrs. Allonby's epigram on the paradoxical power of prohibition — those with more restrictions actually have more freedom."

memes.label: "We have a much better time than they have."
memes.description: "Mrs. Allonby on the relative advantages of women in the social game."

memes.label: "A private letter is the one thing that ought never to be written."
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth on the dangers of written evidence."

memes.label: "One has never heard his name before in the whole course of one's life, which speaks volumes for a man, nowadays."
memes.description: "Lady Caroline's epigram on respectability — a Wildean paradox that equates obscurity with virtue."

memes.label: "No woman should have a memory."
memes.description: "Mrs. Allonby's observation on the burden of female memory in a society that punishes women for their pasts."

memes.label: "Nothing is so dangerous as being too modern."
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth on the perils of modernity — the dandy's paradoxical conservatism."

memes.label: "One should never trust a woman who tells one her real age."
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth on the relationship between honesty and female identity — a wit that doubles as social commentary."

memes.label: "Nowadays it is only the unreadable that occurs."
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's dismissal of modern journalism and literature — a line that reflects Wilde's own views on the press."

memes.label: "The one advantage of playing with fire is that one never gets even singed."
memes.description: "Mrs. Allonby's epigram on the double standard of risk — those who know how to play with danger escape unharmed, while the naive get burned."

memes.label: "\"Do you know, I don't believe in the existence of Puritan women?\""
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's assertion that there is no such thing as a Puritan woman — a classic Wildean inversion that reveals the dandy's view of female nature."

memes.label: "\"It is the problem of slavery. And we are trying to solve it by amusing the slaves.\""
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's devastating epigram on gender relations as slavery — a line that exposes social injustice while maintaining the dandy's characteristic ironic distance."

memes.label: "The soul is born old but grows young. That is the comedy of life."
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's paradoxical meditation on aging — an inversion of the conventional wisdom that youth is the prelude to experience."

memes.label: "There are many things women should never forgive"
memes.description: "Hester Worsley's moral declaration — the ethical turning point of the play that authorizes Mrs. Arbuthnot's refusal."

memes.label: "But what world says that, Lady Stutfield? It must be the next world. This world and I are on excellent terms."
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's most famous epigram — a dandy's declaration of amoral worldly success."

memes.label: "Thoroughly sensible woman, your mother, Gerald."
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's assessment of Mrs. Arbuthnot — an apparently casual compliment that carries enormous dramatic weight given their shared history."

memes.label: "\"We are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars.\""
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's most famous aphorism on ambition and aspiration — a line that has transcended its dramatic context to become one of Wilde's best-known epigrams."
aliases: we are all in the gutter

memes.label: "I was a woman of no importance"
memes.description: "Mrs. Arbuthnot's quiet statement that gives the play its title — a line that transforms self-description into social indictment."

memes.label: "Women are always on the side of morality, public and private."
memes.description: "Mr. Kelvil's moral pronouncement on women and morality — a sententious observation that the play's action systematically undermines."

memes.label: "All women become like their mothers. That is their tragedy."
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth's cruel epigram on mothers — a line that cuts to the play's thematic heart, spoken to the very room that contains the mother of his own child."

memes.label: "Women kneel so gracefully; men don't."
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth on the aesthetics of worship — a line that reveals the dandy's conflation of religion with performance."

memes.label: "A very wonderful opening for so young a man as you are, Mr. Arbuthnot."
memes.description: "Lady Hunstanton's celebration of Gerald Arbuthnot's appointment — the line that sets the entire plot machinery in motion."

memes.label: "The world was made for men and not for women."
memes.description: "Lady Stutfield's lament — a simple statement that crystallizes the play's critique of Victorian gender roles."

memes.label: "Young women of the present day make it the sole object of their lives to be always playing with fire."
memes.description: "Lord Illingworth on the modern young woman — a characteristically ambiguous pronouncement that is part admiration, part warning."

memes.label: "Rebellion"
memes.description: "The acts of rebellion in Wilde's play — from Hester's verbal defiance to Mrs. Arbuthnot's final refusal to accept the terms of a corrupt society."

memes.label: Redemption
memes.description: "The theme of redemption in A Woman of No Importance — who can be redeemed, by whom, and at what cost."

memes.label: "Religion"
memes.description: "The role of religion in Wilde's play — the Archdeacon, the moral language characters invoke, and the gap between religious profession and behaviour."

memes.label: Sacrifice
memes.description: "The theme of sacrifice — particularly maternal sacrifice — as the moral counterweight to the play's dandy amorality."

memes.label: "Scandal"
memes.description: "The role of scandal as the mechanism of social control in Victorian society — the fear of exposure that governs behaviour and the asymmetry of its consequences for men and women."

memes.label: "The Scenes of the Play"
memes.description: "Setting descriptions for the four acts of the play."

memes.label: "Secrecy and the Past"
memes.description: "The role of the past as an inescapable force in A Woman of No Importance — how secrets shape the present and how the revelation of hidden history drives the drama."

memes.label: "The secret of life"
memes.description: "A multi-character epigram exchange in Act III where four characters offer competing definitions of the secret of life, culminating in Lord Illingworth's nihilistic rebuttal."

memes.label: "Sir John Pontefract"
memes.description: "Lady Caroline Pontefract's quiet, long-suffering husband — a minor figure whose few lines epitomize the henpecked aristocratic male in Wilde's social satire."

memes.label: "Social Performance"
memes.description: "The theatricality of social life in Wilde's play — how characters perform their identities, manage their surfaces, and navigate a world in which being and seeming have parted company."

memes.label: The Double Standard
memes.description: "The Victorian sexual double standard — punishing women for transgressions that men commit with impunity — as the central moral critique of A Woman of No Importance."

memes.label: "The English Country House"
memes.description: "The country house as both setting and symbol — a microcosm of English society where the rigid rituals of aristocratic life contain and constrain the drama."

memes.label: The Fallen Woman
memes.description: "The Victorian literary trope of the 'fallen woman' — a woman ruined by sexual transgression — and Wilde's radical revision of it in A Woman of No Importance."

memes.label: "The Ideal Man"
memes.description: "The Ideal Man as a concept of Wildean satire — Mrs. Allonby's celebrated description of the perfect male companion as a figure who embodies both the desires and the absurdities of the society that imagines him."

memes.label: The New Woman
memes.description: "The late Victorian cultural figure of the 'New Woman' — educated, independent, and challenging traditional gender roles — as refracted through the female characters of A Woman of No Importance."

memes.label: The Picture Gallery
memes.description: "The picture gallery at Hunstanton Chase where Act III takes place — the space of confrontation, revelation, and the play's most dramatic moral crisis."

memes.label: "The Puritan"
memes.description: "The figure of the Puritan in Wilde's play — associated with Hester Worsley and with a particular kind of female moral seriousness that the dandies mock but cannot dismiss."

memes.label: "The Self-Made Man"
memes.description: "The figure of the self-made man in Wilde's play — represented by Gerald Arbuthnot's career ambitions and contrasted with Lord Illingworth's inherited position."

memes.label: The Shires
memes.description: "The rural English region where A Woman of No Importance is set — the country-house district that serves as both physical setting and social world."

memes.label: "The 'Woman of No Importance'"
memes.description: "Wilde's central ironic device — the woman whom society dismisses as 'of no importance' proves to be the moral center of the drama, possessing more substance than all the 'important' people combined."

memes.label: "Truth and Lies"
memes.description: "The central tension between truth and concealment in A Woman of No Importance — how the suppression of truth structures the play's society, its characters' lives, and its dramatic resolution."

memes.label: "Victorian Morality"
memes.description: "Wilde's dissection of Victorian morality as a system of appearances rather than principles — where public virtue masks private vice and where the double standard between men and women is the structural foundation of 'respectable' society."

memes.label: Wealth and Status
memes.description: "The role of wealth and social status in defining character and determining outcomes in A Woman of No Importance."

memes.label: "Wit and Epigram"
memes.description: "The epigram as Wilde's primary dramatic tool in A Woman of No Importance — where wit serves simultaneously as entertainment, social critique, and psychological defense."

relations.label: After a good dinner one can forgive anybody

memes.label: "Women's Agency"
memes.description: "The question of female autonomy in Wilde's play — which women have the power to choose their own lives, and at what cost, in a society structured by male power and female reputation."

memes.label: "Wrockley"
memes.description: "The village where Mrs. Arbuthnot lives in quiet obscurity — the space of domestic virtue and hidden suffering that stands in contrast to the glittering world of Hunstanton Chase."

memes.label: Wrockley Sitting-Room
memes.description: "The sitting-room at Mrs. Arbuthnot's house in Wrockley where Act IV takes place — the private space where the play's moral reckoning occurs."

memes.label: Youth and Age
memes.description: "The generational tension in A Woman of No Importance — between the idealism of youth and the compromised wisdom of age."
